title: A genome-scale CRISPRi perturbation atlas of human induced pluripotent stem cells - Nature Biotechnology
text:
AbstractComprehensively mapping the relationship between genotype and phenotype offers essential insights into how a cell’s state arises from its genetic components. Toward this goal, we generated an expressed genome-scale CRISPRi perturbation cell atlas in KOLF2.1J human induced pluripotent stem cells, mapping transcriptional phenotypes associated with 11,692 perturbed genes across >2.5 million single cells. Using correlations among perturbed phenotypes, we created a cell map of the pluripotent state, demonstrating rich recapitulation of functionally related protein complexes. We then explored the atlas to uncover metabolic factor ZBTB41 and pluripotency regulator RNF7, validating their functions through metabolic tracing, immunofluorescence and protein–protein interaction assays. Lastly, we leveraged the atlas to generate a genome-scale screen of A-to-I RNA-editing modulators assayed through direct transcriptome-wide RNA editing, uncovering and mechanistically validating DBR1 as a potent regulator. Taken together, our data provide a comprehensive resource for interrogating the regulatory networks governing pluripotency, which is accessible at https://y-doctor.github.io/KOLF2.1J_Perturbation_Cell_Atlas/.

Data availability

FASTQ files can be found through the Sequence Read Archive under BioProject PRJNA1173491. The anndata file containing the aggregated 10x feature-barcode matrix can be found on Figshare (https://doi.org/10.25452/figshare.plus.27261219)108. An online web browser for the dataset can be found on GitHub (https://y-doctor.github.io/KOLF2.1J_Perturbation_Cell_Atlas/), with further information on data availability. All other data supporting the findings of this study are available within the article and its Supplementary Information. Source data are provided with this paper.
Code availability

Analysis pipeline code and Jupyter notebooks used for analysis are available through GitHub (https://github.com/y-doctor/KOLF2.1J_Perturbation_Cell_Atlas/).

Additional informationPublisher’s note Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.Extended dataExtended Data Fig. 1 Perturb-Seq of Chromatin Modifiers and Metabolic Enzymes in KOLF2.1J hiPSCs.(a) Perturb-Seq pilot screens experimental scheme. KOLF2.1J iPSCs constitutively expressing KRABZIM3-dCas9 were transduced with a sgRNA library targeting two gene sets: (i) a panel of 108 chromatin modifying genes and (ii) a panel of 97 genes involved in metabolism. 108 non-targeting control (NTC) sgRNA were included. Following 2 day recovery, cells receiving perturbations were selected via puromycin for 2 days. On day 4, cells were harvested for single cell RNA sequencing. (Created using Biorender https://BioRender.com/f8grmmb) (b-l) correspond to results from the chromatin modifiers screen, while (m-q) correspond to results from the metabolic enzymes screen. (b) Number of cells per perturbation, indicating that 92% of perturbations (sgRNA) have more than 25 associated cells. (c) On-target knockdown per perturbation, indicating that 65% of perturbations demonstrate repression >30%. (d) Energy distance permutation testing of perturbations vs NTC cells. (e) Relationship between energy distance and number of induced differentially expressed genes (DEGs) per perturbation. Perturbations with significance for both metrics are retained. sgRNA are combined per gene to form Strong Perturbations. (f) Number of up- and down- regulated DEGs per Strong Perturbation, identified via DESeq2. (g) Waterfall plot ranking Strong Perturbations on their average combined rank of energy distance and DEGs, with the top 10 labeled. (h) Pairwise correlation matrix of pseudo bulked transcriptional profiles of strong perturbations. (i) Exploration of alternate methods of clustering perturbations (See Methods). (j) Annotated Minimum Distortion Embedding (MDE) of Strong Perturbation correlation matrix. (k) UMAP density visualization of perturbed and NTC cells (top), DNMT1 and UHRF1 (bottom). (l) Mechanism by which URHF1 recruits DNMT1 to regulate hemimethylated DNA. Modified from Arita et al.18 (Created using Biorender https://BioRender.com/rvwc39u) (m, n) Effect of loading variable number of cells per 10X channel on yield of cells with 1 sgRNA assigned for metabolic enzymes Perturb-Seq dataset. (o) Energy distance permutation testing of perturbations vs NTC cells. (p) Pairwise correlation matrix of pseudo bulked transcriptional profiles of Strong Perturbations. (q) UMAP density visualization of perturbed and NTC cells (top), MTOR (bottom).Extended Data Fig. 2 Data Analysis Pipeline PASTA (Perturbation Atlas Sanitization, Triage, and Analysis).Plots correspond to data from expressed genome wide Perturb-Seq assay. See Methods for full details. Data is first sanitized (a-f), removing outlier cells and non-effective perturbations. Next, perturbations are triaged, removing low-priority perturbations that do not measurably cause cell state alterations (g-k). Finally, analysis is performed to develop a cellular map. (l, Fig.2). (a) sgRNA are assigned per cell using 10x Genomics guide calling algorithm. Cells without exactly 1 sgRNA are filtered. (b) Genes not detected in any single cells are removed. (c) Cells are filtered based on standard QC metrics proposed by Heumos et al.103 (d) Sanity check of standard QC metrics. (e) Filtering of spurious NTC cells. NTC cells are batch-aligned via Harmony105 and filtered via Isolation Forest109 assuming a 30% contamination fraction. (f) Perturbations (sgRNA) with less than 25 associated cells and achieving less than 30% on-target knockdown are removed as proposed by Replogle et al.36 (g) Energy distance permutation test vs NTC via scPerturb15 (h) (Left) Differential expression test of perturbations vs NTC cells via DESeq2. (Right) Distribution of number of DEGs for each NTC sgRNA vs. all other NTC cells is used to set a FDR on the # of DEGs identified. (i) Perturbations with both a significant energy distance test result and more DEGs than the NTC FDR baseline are selected. Perturbations are then combined per targeted gene. (j, k) Energy distance testing and # of DEGs filtering is repeated at the gene level. ‘Strong Perturbations’ are defined as those which pass all filtering from steps a-k. (l) Strong Perturbations are prioritized based on the average of their energy distance and # of DEGs rank.Extended Data Fig. 3 Further Analysis Related to CRISPRi Fitness Screening.(a) Top 5 GO terms for enriched/depleted perturbations related to Fig.1(c). (b) Fitness screen analysis Z-normalizing to distribution of NTC sgRNA rather than all perturbations (related to Fig.1c). (c) Fitness screen analysis Z-normalizing to distribution of NTC sgRNA rather than all perturbations displays the same pathways as when Z-normalizing to all (related to Fig.1d). (c) Pathways and processes enrichment analysis of DepMap exclusive essential genes (related to Fig.1d). (e) Pathways and processes enrichment analysis of essential genes shared between DepMap and KOLF2.1Js (related to Fig.1d). (f, g) Fitness Z-score at Day 6 (D6) and Day 14 (D14) in KOLF2.1J KRABZIM3-dCas9 expressing cells at the perturbation level. Each dot represents a different gRNA, 3 gRNA/perturbation and 478 gRNAs for NTC conditions. Red bars are perturbations with z-score < −1 at day 14, blue bars are perturbations with z-score>1 at day 14. Error Bars represent SD and asterisks indicate significant differences compared to sg-NTC condition (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, ****p < 0.0001) (h-k) Gene expression assessed by qPCR between NTC (sg-NTC) and targeting gRNA (sg-Gene) in transduced KRABZIM3-dCas9 KOLF2.1J for n = 24 genes (h) including Mitochondrial ribosome large subunit members (i) Stress response genes GCN1/GCN2 (j) and Transcription/Epigenetic Factors (k). Error bars represent SD and asterisks indicate significant differences (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, ****p < 0.0001, n = 2 biological replicates). (l) Correlation of gene-level Z-scores at day 14 between KOLF2 hiPSCs from this study and KOLF2 hiPSCs from Usluer et al.29). Fitness enriching perturbations are identified by Z > 1 in both datasets, moderate essential Z < −1, strong essential Z < −2. (m) Venn diagram showing overlap between strong essential genes. (n) Pathways and processes enrichment analysis of strong essential genes shared between this work and Usluer et al. dataset.Source dataExtended Data Fig. 4 Analysis of Strong Perturbation Clusters and Transcriptomic Effects.(a) (Left) UMAP of single cell transcriptional profile of Strong Perturbations colored by Leiden Clustering. (Middle, Right) Density of UMAP embedding for Perturbed vs. NTC cells. (b) (Left) Pairwise correlation matrix of pseudo bulked transcriptional profiles of Strong Perturbations, subset to those identified in a HDBSCAN cluster. (Right) Annotation and membership of perturbations forming HDBSCAN clusters. (c) STRING PPI network of HDBSCAN Clusters 20 and 21 (Pluripotency related). (d) Density of UMAP embedding for selected HDBSCAN clusters. (e) Gene expression relative to NTC for lineage specifying gene lists curated by Tchieu et al.110 and Loh et al.111. Strong Perturbations with |Z| > 0.5 for any marker are shown (similar to analysis by Replogle et al. Fig. 4). (P) Pluripotency, (PS) Primitive Streak, (DE) Definitive Endoderm, (M) Mesoderm, (E) Ectoderm.Extended Data Fig. 5 Summarizing Strong Perturbation and Feature Gene Cluster Relationships.(a) (Left) Pairwise correlation matrix of Feature Genes, subset to those identified in a HDBSCAN cluster. (Right) Annotation of Feature Gene HDBSCAN clusters. (b) STRING PPI networks of selected Feature Gene HDBSCAN clusters. (c) Summary clustermap of Strong Perturbation and Feature Gene HDBSCAN clusters. Each box (row i, column j) represents the average Z-score of all Strong Perturbations in column j across all Feature Genes in row i.Extended Data Fig. 6 Visualization of Strong Perturbation Relationships via Minimum Distortion Embedding (MDE).(a) MDE of Strong Perturbation correlation matrix colored by Leiden clusters. (b) MDE map colored by HDBSCAN clusters, where members of the same HDBSCAN cluster are given the same color. Strong Perturbations not assigned a cluster are shown in gray. (c) MDE map colored by genes in CORUM Links (see Methods). Each blue dot is a gene that is a member of a CORUM complex. Blue lines are drawn between pairs of genes within the same CORUM complex. (d) Overlay of plot (b) on (c). (e) MDE ‘skeleton’ map of KOLF 2.1J hiPSCs derived from (a). Each node represents Leiden cluster centroids, with the size of the node corresponding to the number of cluster members. Nodes are connected by edges to their 3 nearest neighbors. Nodes are labeled based on enriched GO/CORUM/STRING terms. (f-i) Skeleton MDE nodes colored by median fitness D6 Z-Score (f), median fitness D14 Z-Score (g), median # of DEGs (h), and median energy distance (i).Extended Data Fig. 7 SEC-MS Validation of LAMTOR and Mitochondrial Translation Complexes in KOLF2.1J hiPSCs.(a) Experimental scheme for size exclusion chromatography coupled with mass spectrometry (SEC-MS) validation experiment. Proteins were extracted from wild-type (WT) KOLF2.1Js and separated by size-exclusion chromatography. Fractions were analyzed by mass spectrometry to identify and quantify proteins by Spectronaut, followed by protein-protein interaction (PPI) analysis yielding interaction networks based on cosine similarity of fractionation profiles. (b) STRING Protein-Protein interaction network of Strong Perturbation HDBSCAN Cluster 29 (Ragulator Complex). (c) Fractionation profile of LAMTOR2/3/4/5. (d) PPI Network predicted from SEC-MS data by PCprophet. (e) STRING Protein-Protein interaction network of Strong Perturbation HDBSCAN Cluster 26 (Mitochondrial Translation). HDBSCAN Cluster 26 contains MDE Clusters 8, 10, 14, 20, and 45 which are indicated on the skeleton MDE with blue arrows. (f) Cosine similarity clustermap of Strong Perturbation fractionation profiles for indicated MDE clusters.Extended Data Fig. 8 Analysis of Induced Transcriptional Profiles by POU5F1/RNF7 Knockdown.(a) POU5F1 and RNF7 knockdown induced by two independent RNF7 sgRNA assessed by bulk-RNA seq (****p < 0.001 per DESeq2). (b) Volcano plot of DEGs (|LFC| > 1, adj. p < 0.05) for RNF7 sgRNA-1, with overlaid DEGs from RNF7 sgRNA-2 (purple), demonstrating that a majority of top DEGs from sgRNA-1 are also identified as DEGs in sgRNA-2. (c) Enriched GO terms (determined via STRINGDB function enrichment tool) from overlap of DEGs between RNF7 sgRNA-1 and sgRNA-2. All further assessments of RNF7 use RNF7 sgRNA-1. (d) Enlarged brightfield images representative of KRABZIM3-dCas9 KOLF2.1J cells with knockdown to RNF7 and POU5F1. Scale bars correspond to 75 microns. Three fields are shown for each condition. (e) Pearson correlation of DEGs identified in Perturb-seq with single-plex bulk RNA-Seq validation for sg-POU5F1 and sg-RNF7 to sg-NTC in KOLF2.1J. (f) Volcano plots of sg-POU5F1 and sg-RNF7 bulk RNA-seq. Purple dots highlight DEGs common to both perturbations. (g) Venn diagram illustrating the overlap of DEGs between sg-POU5F1 and sg-RNF7 knockdowns compared to sg-NTC. (h) Gene Set Enrichment Analysis (GSEA) plots comparing each knockdown condition (sg-POU5F1, left; sg-RNF7, right) against the sg-NTC control, showing enrichment of ‘Primitive Endoderm Cell Embryo’ and depletion of ‘Epiblast Cell Embryo’ gene signatures. NES: Normalized Enrichment Score. (i) Bar plots showing the top five significantly enriched Reactome pathways from GSEA, ranked by NES, for sg-POU5F1 (top) and sg-RNF7 (bottom). (j) Heatmap showing expression changes of key genes involved in pluripotency maintenance (left) and definitive endoderm formation (right). Values represent Log2 fold change from bulk RNA-seq data.Source dataExtended Data Fig. 9 BioID-Based Mapping of RNF7 and POU5F1 Protein Interactomes in KOLF2.1Js.(a) Schematic of the BioID workflow. KOLF2 hiPSCs were engineered to express doxycycline-inducible BirA*-tagged constructs (GFP-BirA*, RNF7-BirA*, or POU5F1-BirA*). Following induction and addition of biotin, cells were lysed, biotinylated proteins were captured by streptavidin pulldown, on-bead tryptic digestion performed, and peptides were identified by LC-MS/MS. (Created using Biorender https://BioRender.com/67kw34i) (b) Western blot analysis of KOLF2.1J cells expressing GFP-BirA*, RNF7-BirA*, or POU5F1-BirA*, induced for 24 h with 1 µg/mL doxycycline and 1 µM biotin. Lysates were probed with streptavidin-HRP to detect biotinylated proteins (top), anti-Myc to confirm BirA* fusion protein expression (middle), and GAPDH as a loading control (bottom). (c) Venn diagram showing overlap of high-confidence prey proteins (FDR ≤ 1%) identified in GFP-BirA*, RNF7-BirA*, and POU5F1-BirA* experiments. Numbers indicate unique and shared proteins across the three conditions. (d) Log2 fold-change (LFC) of spectral counts for RNF7-BirA* versus GFP-BirA* from replicate 1 is plotted against replicate 2. Pearson correlation coefficient (r = 0.79). (e) Enrichment Z-score of RNF7-BirA* versus GFP-BirA*; and 210 significantly enriched preys are indicated (blue dots). (f) Gene Ontology Cellular Component (GO:CC) enrichment for RNF7-BioID 210 preys. (g) Spectral counts of established RNF7 interactors (CUL5, UBE2F, UBE2D3, UBE2F) across GFP-BirA*, RNF7-BirA*, and POU5F1-BirA* experiments. Error bars represent standard deviation of two replicates. (h) Log2 fold-change (LFC) of spectral counts for POU5F1-BirA* versus GFP-BirA* from replicate 1 is plotted against replicate 2. Pearson correlation coefficient (r = 0.88). (i) Enrichment Z-score of POU5F1-BirA* versus GFP-BirA*; 164 significantly enriched preys are indicated (blue dots). (j) Gene Ontology Cellular Component (GO:CC) enrichment for RNF7-BioID 164 preys. (k-n) Comparative spectral count analysis (mean ± SD, N = 2) of GFP-BirA*, RNF7-BirA*, and POU5F1-BirA* BioID datasets for: (k) established POU5F1 interactors SALL4, SALL3, ARID1B, CBX7, TCF7L1, SMARCC1, SMARCA4, PAXIP1 and FOXD1; (l) core MLL3/4 complex subunits KMT2D, KMT2C, KDM6A, UTY, PAXIP1 and NCOA6; (m) β-catenin–TCF transcriptional complex members TCF7L1, TCF7L2, TLE1, TLE3, LDB1 and BCL9; and (n) SWI/SNF chromatin-remodeling complex subunits ARID1A, ARID1B, SMARCC1, SMARCC2, SMARCD1, SMARCD3, SMARCA4, PBRM1 and DPF2.Source dataExtended Data Fig. 10 Additional Analysis of Regulators of RNA Editing in KOLF2.1J hiPSCs.(a) A graphical representation of the regulatory interactions between Strong Perturbations. Each Strong Perturbation is a node, and a directed outgoing edge is drawn from a node and every other node that is a DEG of that node (that is is regulated by it). Node sizes correspond to their PageRank node importance on the reverse graph, and the top 50 Regulators (as identified by pagerank) are highlighted. (b) Waterfall plot of regulators as identified by PageRank. (c) Barplot of Top 30 regulators as identified by PageRank. (d) Bar plot showing the number of differentially edited RNA sites at a stricter q threshold (q < 0.01) identified following knockdown of ADAR and DBR1. Sites with increased editing relative to sg-NTC (wEI ≥ 0) are shown in green; sites with decreased editing (wEI < 0) are shown in red.
